questions.question: Approximate $\int_{0}^{2} (x^3 + 1) \, dx$ using a right Riemann sum with 4 subintervals.
Use a left Riemann sum with $n = 5$ to approximate $\int_{0}^{5} \sqrt{x} \, dx$.

The table gives values of $g(t)$. Use a midpoint Riemann sum with 2 subintervals to estimate $\int_{0}^{12} g(t) \, dt$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} t & 0 & 3 & 6 & 9 & 12 \\ \hline g(t) & 8 & 5 & 4 & 6 & 7 \end{array}$
Approximate the area under $f(x) = e^x$ on $[0,1]$ using a left Riemann sum with 5 subintervals. (Leave answer in terms of $e$.)
Water flows into a tank at rate $r(t) = 20 - 2t$ L/min. Use a right Riemann sum with 4 subintervals to estimate the total water added from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$ minutes.
For $f(x) = x^2 - 2x$ on $[-1,3]$, use a midpoint Riemann sum with 4 subintervals.

Given the table, use a left Riemann sum with 4 subintervals to approximate $\int_{0}^{8} f(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c} x & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 \\ \hline f(x) & 5 & 3 & 4 & 2 & 1 \end{array}$
Approximate $\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos x \, dx$ using a right Riemann sum with $n = 3$.

The speed of a runner (m/s) is measured every 0.5 seconds: $t=0:0, 0.5:2, 1.0:4, 1.5:3, 2.0:1$. Use a midpoint sum with 4 subintervals to estimate the distance run from $t=0$ to $t=2$. (Assume linear interpolation for midpoints.)
questions.explanation: $\Delta t = 0.5$; midpoints: 0.25, 0.75, 1.25, 1.75. Interpolated speeds: 1, 3, 3.5, 2. Sum = 9.5, times 0.5 = 4.75 m.

questions.question: For $f(x) = \frac{1}{x+1}$ on $[0,3]$, use a left Riemann sum with $n = 6$ equal subintervals.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered Riemann sum approximations.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! You understand the basics of Riemann sums. Review midpoint calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on identifying endpoints and calculating $\Delta x$ correctly.
scoreMessages.0: Review the definition of Riemann sums: $\sum f(x_i^*) \Delta x$.
